10. Officers recalled from leave granted under the new leave rules will be given free passage to India, or not, according to the Transport Regulations in force at the time.

11. Advances of pay to officers proceeding on and returning from leave out of India will be granted as heretofore.

(Published in India Army Circulars, December 1886, Clause 197.)

proper authority, and then on special and urgent grounds.

3. An Officer subject to the leave rules for the Staff Corps on promotion to Major-General will not be granted leave under those rules, and, if permitted to reside in England or elsewhere out of India, will be restricted to the English pay of his rank.

(Published in India Army Circulars, 17th March 1887.)

Para. 7, Clause 113, India Army Circulars, 1886, is cancelled. (See above.)

Officers subject to the leave rules for the Staff Corps who are in the following categories, and who entered the Indian Army or Staff Corps before the 1st July 1881, may elect to reside out of India on the allowances admissible under those rules until succession to the Colonel's allowance or promotion to Major-General, whichever may happen first; or to remain in India on the unemployed Indian pay and allowances of their rank if unemployed.

(a.) Officers vacating regimental commands, remaining in the service for the attainment of the highest retirement pension, or the Colonel's allowance.

(b.) Officers over 55 years of age, who may be required under the rules to vacate Staff, Departmental, or Civil appointments, and for whom other suitable employment cannot be found.

(c.) Officers of the Commissariat, Military Accounts, and Judge Advocate-General's Departments, who vacate their appointments on attaining to 52 years of age.

(d.) Officers who on the 8th August 1884 were holding appointments of unlimited tenure to which they were appointed before the 1st January 1884, and who vacate their appointments on completing seven years' tenure from date of appointment or such further extension as may be granted to enable them to complete 32 years' service for pension not exceeding nine years in all

(e.) Squadron or Wing Commanders who vacate their appointments on attaining 52 years of age.

(f.) Colonels who entered the Staff Corps after the 12th September 1866 and before the 1st July 1881 and Colonels of the General List, after transfer to the Supernumerary List.

The limit of absence from duty imposed by the new leave rules will not be held applicable to these Officers.

ADVANCES OF PAY.

Rules regarding advances of pay to Officers under the Furlough Rules of 1868 and 1875, and the Leave Rules for the Indian Staff Corps, published in Army Circulars, India, 16 April 1886:—

Officers proceeding on furlough to Europe may, if they desire it, receive an advance equivalent to three months' furlough pay.

Officers returning from furlough or from temporary half pay will similarly be granted advances of pay at the following rates, on obtaining permission to return:—

For a Subaltern ... ... ... £65
For a Captain ... ... ... £100
For a Field Officer ... ... ... £150

No advance, however, will be issued to an officer who obtains from the Fund to which he is a subscriber a loan equal to or greater than the advance author-

ized for his rank; and an officer who obtains from his Fund a loan less in amount than such advance will be granted only the difference between the two. Recovery of the advances made in England to be effected in India by five equal monthly instalments at the rate of exchange fixed for the year in which the advance was made.

The following advances on return to duty will be issued to Warrant Officers, on the same conditions:—

To First Class Warrant Officers ... £50
To other Warrant Officers with honorary commissions ... ...
To Second Class Warrant Officers ... £25

* Army Regulations, India, Vol. I., Part I., Article 1613, Clause (c).

INDIAN PENSION REGULATIONS.

(The Rules affecting Commissioned Officers took effect from 1st July 1881.)

STAFF CORPS.

1. Officers who entered the Staff Corps before or on the 12th September 1866, are entitled to pensions and additional pensions at the following rates:—

Ordinary Pension.

Per Annum.
£ s. d.
After 20 years' service for pension ..... 191 12 6
24 " " ..... 292 0 0
28 " " ..... 365 0 0
32 " " ..... 456 5 0
35 " " ..... 600 0 0
38 " " ..... 750 0 0

Additional Pension.

Per Annum.
£
After 20 years' service from date of 1st commission... 97
21 " " ..... 105
22 " " ..... 115
23 " " ..... 126
24 " " ..... 138
25 " " ..... 152
26 " " ..... 168
27 " " ..... 186
28 " " ..... 206
29 " " ..... 230
30 " " ..... 257
31 " " ..... 289
32 " " ..... 327

† Additional pensions are not granted with these two pensions.

2. Officers who entered the Staff Corps after the 12th September 1866, but before the 1st July 1881, are entitled to pensions at the following rates:—

Per Annum.
After 20 years' service for pension £ 250
24 " " 365
28 " " 500
32 " " 700
38 " " 750

3. Officers who entered the Staff Corps after the 1st July 1881, may retire on the rates laid down in paragraph 2, with the exception of the pension of £750 for 38 years' service. These Officers are subject to compulsory retirement, in the grades of Lieutenant-Colonel, Colonel, or General Officer, on account of age or continuous non-employment. General Officers thus retired will receive—

£
In any grade, at 62 years of age..... 800 a-year.
Lieutenant-Generals, at 67 years of age 900 "
Generals, at 67 years of age..... 1,000 "

They may retire voluntarily—

  • In any grade, at 60, on £750 a-year,
  • Lieutenant-General, at 65, on £850 a-year,
  • General, at 65, on £950 a-year,
  • No General Officer receiving less, on retirement, than £700 a-year.

4. Officers who entered the Staff Corps before the 1st July, 1881, provided they have relinquished their claim to Colonel's allowance, may claim the benefit of the above pensions for General Officers.

CAVALRY AND INFANTRY.

5. Officers of the local service, that is, under the old conditions of appointment, are entitled to the scales of ordinary and additional pension fixed in paragraph 1 for the Officers who entered the Staff Corps up to the 12th September 1866. They are also allowed, after 22 years' service for pension, provided they have not elected Staff Corps rules for pay and promotion, to retire on the full pay pension of their respective substantive ranks, viz:—

Per Annum.
£ s. d.
Major 292 0 0
Lieutenant-Colonel 365 0 0

with additional pensions given in paragraph 1.

6. Officers of the General Lists, that is, those under the conditions of appointment which came into force in 1859, may retire on the scale given in paragraph 2 for Officers who have entered the Staff Corps after the 12th September 1866.

7. Officers of Cavalry and Infantry, provided they have relinquished their claim to Colonel's allowances, may also claim the benefits of the pensions for General Officers given in paragraph 3.

MEDICAL SERVICE.

8. The scale of pension for Medical Officers is as follows:—

Per Annum.
After 17 years' service for pension £ 292
20 " " 365
25 " " 500
30 " " 700

with extra pensions of £350 and £250 respectively to Officers who have completed a five years' tour of duty as Surgeon-General or Deputy Surgeon-General.

9. A Medical Officer who entered the service before the 13th January, 1860, and who is compulsorily retired on attaining the age of 65 or 68 years, may, however, retire under previous regulations, i.e., on the next higher pension to that to which he would have been entitled on the old scales. The only "next higher" rates which give any pecuniary advantage are those of £456 per annum after 24 years' service for pension, and £550 after 27, 28, or 29 years' service for pension.

VETERINARY DEPARTMENT.

10. The following are the rates of pension for Veterinary Surgeons—

Per Annum.
After 20 years' service for pension £ 200
25 " " 350
30 " " 450

HALF PAY.

11. Combatant Officers receive the same rates of half pay as those granted to Officers of the same ranks in the British service.*

12. Medical Officers receive the British rates of half pay of the corresponding military rank.

SERVICE FOR PENSION.

13. Service for pension (except as shown in the following paragraphs) commences from date of arrival in India and includes all leave and furlough taken after that date.

14. Officers who were appointed from Addiscombe count the period passed at the seminary after attaining the age of 16 years.

15. Medical Officers count as service the period passed at the Army Medical School.

16. Officers appointed to the Staff Corps from the British Service are allowed to count service out of India before joining the Staff Corps, to an extent not exceeding two years in any case.

17. Officers who have entered the Staff Corps from the British Service only become entitled to pension after one of the fixed periods specified when not less than one half of the period has been passed in the Staff Corps.

18. Under the Furlough Regulations of 1796 all periods of leave within Indian limits reckon as service, all absence beyond Indian limits being deducted.

19. Under the Furlough Regulations of 1854, or those of 1863, the following periods of furlough and leave reckon as service:—

Combatant Officers.

2 years in 20 years' service.
3 " " 24 " "
4 " " 25 " "
5 " " 32 " "
6 " " 38 " "

Medical Officers and Veterinary Surgeons.

2 years in 17 years' service.
3 " " 20 " "
4 " " 25 " "
5 " " 30 " "

20. Under the Furlough Regulations of 1875, all leave within Indian limits, and all furlough earned by length of service (i.e., one year for every five years' service in India,) reckon as service.

21. Under certain conditions, leave rendered necessary by wounds or by illness solely contracted on field service is allowed to reckon as service.

22. No deduction on account of leave of absence is made from the periods of service for the additional pensions specified in paragraph 1.

COMMUTATION OF PENSION.

23. An Officer retiring under the above regulations will be allowed, under certain conditions,† to commute a portion, not exceeding one-third, of his pension, which commutation may take place on retirement or at any time subsequent thereto.

WARRANT OFFICERS.

(These rules took effect from the 1st Sept., 1881.)

1. All Warrant Officers on the Establishment on 16th September, 1881, are allowed the option of electing the old or the new scale of pensions at any time previous to retirement. The distinction between the invalid pension, which can be drawn only in India, and the retiring pension, is abolished.

2. The periods of service entitling warrant officers to retiring pensions are as follows:—

  • 24 years' total service in Europe and India, including 16 years in the department,
  • 26 years' total service in Europe and India, including 14 years in the department,
  • 28 years' total service in Europe and India, including 12 years in the department.

3. The retiring pensions are—

England or the Colonies. India.
Per annum. Per mensem
Deputy Commissary £216 Rs. 200
Assistant Commissary 144 140
Deputy Assistant Commissary 108 100
Conductor 90 85
Sub-Conductor 72 70

4. A warrant officer of any grade, wishing to retire after 31 years' total service, receives a pension of £54

* As laid down in Royal Warrant of 10th June 1884.

† See next page.

per annum in England or the Colonies, or Rs. 50 per mensem in India.

5. A warrant officer declared by a medical board to be unfit for further service in India, after completion of half the periods of service, army and departmental, laid down in paragraph 3, receives—

England or the Colonies. India. Per annum.
Per annum. mensem.
Deputy Commissary ... £172 Rs. 160
Assistant Commissary ... ... 115 ... 110
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... ... 86 ... 80
Conductor ... ... 72 ... 68
Sub-Conductor ... ... 58 ... 56

6. If he has served less than half the prescribed periods, but more than 14 years altogether, he receives—

England or the Colonies. India. Per annum.
Per annum. mensem.
Deputy Commissary ... £115 Rs. 110
Assistant Commissary ... ... 86 ... 80
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... ... 72 ... 68
Conductor ... ... 58 ... 56
Sub-Conductor ... ... 43 ... 40

7. If he has not served 14 years altogether, his case will be specially considered.

8. A warrant officer, whose disqualification for further service in India has been caused by intemperance or irregular habits, receives, if he has served half the periods of service, army and departmental, laid down in paragraph 3—

England or the Colonies. India. Per annum.
Per annum. mensem.
Deputy Commissary ... £130 Rs. 120
Assistant Commissary ... ... 86 ... 85
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... ... 65 ... 60
Conductor ... ... 54 ... 50
Sub-Conductor ... ... 43 ... 42

9. If he has served less than half the prescribed periods, but more than 14 years altogether, he receives—

England or the Colonies. India. Per annum.
Per annum. mensem.
Deputy Commissary ... £86 Rs. 85
Assistant Commissary ... ... 65 ... 60
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... ... 54 ... 50
Conductor ... ... 43 ... 42
Sub-Conductor ... ... 32 ... 30

10. If he has not served 14 years altogether, his case will be specially considered.

11. Retirement is compulsory at the age of 55 years; and all warrant officers of that age in the service on 16th September, 1881, will be required to retire at the expiration of one year from that date.

12. All warrant officers on the establishment on 16th September, 1881, will be allowed the option of electing the old or the new scale of pensions at any time previous to retirement.

13. Pension, according to the present scale and conditions, is admissible to the widow of a warrant officer who may die, either while in the service or on the Pension establishment, provided, in the latter case, that she married the deceased warrant officer previous to his retiring from the army or being invalided.

REGULATIONS FOR THE COMMUTATION OF PENSIONS OF OFFICERS OF THE INDIAN SERVICE.

1. These Regulations shall be applicable only to Officers retired on or after the 1st July, 1881.

2. An Officer may be allowed to commute a portion, not exceeding one-third, of his pension, which commutation may take place on retirement, or at any time subsequent thereto.

3. An Officer in Europe desirous of commuting must make application, in writing, to the Under Secretary of State for India, stating his desire so to commute, and furnishing information as to his age, rank, and the amount of his pension.

4. If the Secretary of State for India in Council shall approve the commutation, the Officer will be so informed, and his application will be forwarded to the Standing Medical Board at the India Office.

5. The Board will require the Officer to give such information as to his health and habits as is usual in

the case of persons proposing to assure their lives in assurance companies, and he will be required to submit himself for personal examination by the Board at the India Office.

6. An Officer in India desirous of commuting must address the authorities of his Presidency. He will be required to go through similar forms, Boards, which will, when practicable, be presided over by the chief Civil Medical Administrative Officer of the Government, being formed in the Presidency towns of Calcutta, Madras, and Bombay, under orders of the respective Governments.

7. The basis of commutation, subject to an addition of years of age in the case of impaired lives, shall be the following table, the age of the Officer being reckoned at the age he will attain on the birthday next succeeding the date upon which the capitalised sum will become payable:—

Age next Birthday. Number of Years' Purchase. Present Value of the Annuity of £100. Age next Birthday. Number of Years' Purchase. Present Value of the Annuity of £100.
£ s. d. £ s. d.
2115.002581,500525111.213561,12171
2214.961111,496235210.981451,098211
2314.925191,4921055310.751331,07528
2414.889691,4881955410.525601,052113
2514.851911,4853105510.303511,03070
2614.808901,48017105610.083631,00873
2714.747461,4741411579.86670936135
2814.679511,467190589.648939641711
2914.605511,460110599.42649942130
3014.525811,452118609.19800919160
3114.440541,44411618.95666895134
3214.350051,43501628.7013287028
3314.252571,42552638.4343584388
3414.147311,414148648.16571816115
3514.034421,403810657.89583789118
3613.914781,39197667.6243776289
3713.790161,37904677.3708273718
3813.660601,36612687.11881711178
3913.527381,352149696.86850686170
4013.388541,338171706.6217066235
4113.243131,32463716.3806863814
4213.085681,308114726.14448614811
4312.920501,29210735.90561590113
4412.744831,27498745.655405651010
4512.558341,255168755.39358539109
4612.360961,236111765.12566512114
4712.146901,2141310774.85132481108
4811.923391,19269784.50565450113
4911.691231,16927794.20762420153
5011.453621,14573803.9147739196

8. The commutation having been sanctioned, the applicant will be informed, and will have the option of withdrawing his application within 30 days after receiving that notice.

9. If the applicant shall not withdraw his application within that period, the decision will be considered final, and the commutation of pension will take place absolutely.

10. In every case the capitalized sum will be payable in India, the sterling amount being converted at the official rate of exchange for the year in which the payment is made.

11. In the case of an officer in Europe, the capitalized sum will be payable at the expiration of two months, and in the case of an officer in India, at the

expiration of one month from the date of the commutation being sanctioned. In each case the officer's pension will be issued in full up to the date of payment, unless any delay has been caused by the officer's own fault or that of his agents.

12. In any case in which a pensioner shall accept public employment, involving the withholding of pension, a deduction will be made from the salary payable to him equal to the amount which would have been abated from the pension if it had not been commuted, and in the event of his becoming entitled to a pension in respect of such employment, the amount of such pension granted to him shall not exceed the amount which he might have received had his pension not been so commuted.

CONDITIONS OF TRANSFER TO TEMPORARY HALF-PAY.

COMMISSIONED OFFICERS.

(Sanctioned by Despatch to Government of India, No. 432, dated 22nd December, 1881.)

Officers placed on temporary half-pay, at the recommendation of the Medical Board of the India Office, shall be examined by the same Board as to their fitness to return to duty every two years, and if still reported unfit at the third examination, that is, after being six years on the temporary half-pay list, they shall be transferred to the retired list on permanent half-pay. An Officer reported to be unfit at the commencement of the second period, i.e., after two years on half-pay, may, however, be permitted voluntarily to retire on permanent half-pay.

Officers unable to appear before the Medical Board at the stated times will be required to furnish a certificate, signed by two medical men, as to their inability. An Officer who is continuously unable to appear will be retired permanently after six years without further report.

Officers who, in view to being restored to the active list, may wish to appear before the Board at other times than those specified, may be permitted to do so. Officers ordered periodically to appear before the Board will receive their travelling expenses and lodging allowance on the Home scale, but those appearing at other times, at their own wish, must do so at their own expense.

WARRANT OFFICERS.

(Sanctioned by Despatch to Government of India, No. 25, dated 24th January 1884.)

A Warrant Officer transferred to temporary half-pay may be permitted to remain on half-pay till his total continuous absence from duty, on furlough and half-pay together, amounts to four years. If then still unfit to return to duty, he must be pensioned on the rate of pension for which he had qualified at the time of being placed on half-pay.

No period spent on half-pay will count as service for Indian pension, nor will a Warrant Officer be eligible for promotion while he is on half-pay. A Warrant Officer replaced on the effective list, on return to duty from half-pay, will be re-employed at once, being borne as a supernumerary in his grade until absorbed by the next vacancy.

Warrant Officers will be required to appear before a Medical Board at the expiration of one year on half-pay, and again, if retained on the half-pay list, at the end of the second year; and those who, in view to being restored to the effective list, may wish to appear before the Board at other times than those specified, may be permitted to do so. Warrant Officers ordered periodically to appear before the Board will be allowed, in this country, their bona fide travelling expenses, and lodging allowance at the rates stated below, but those appearing at other times, and at their own wish, must do so at their own expense.

Rates of Lodging Allowance.

Deputy Commissary ... ... ... ... ... } 2s. 3d. per diem.
Senior Apothecary after 3 years' service in the 1st grade ... ... ... ... ...
Assistant Commissary ... ... ... ... ... } 2s.
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... ... ... ... ...
Senior Apothecary under 3 years' service in the 1st grade ... ... ... ... ... } 1s. 4d.
Conductor ... ... ... ... ...
Sub-Conductor ... ... ... ... ... } 1s. 4d. "
Medical Warrant Officers below the rank of Senior Apothecary ... ... ... ... ...

ISSUE OF FURLOUGH AND RETIRED PAY.

Military Furlough and Retired Pay is made up at the India Office to the 31st January, 30th April, 31st July and 31st October, and is payable on the 1st of the following month.

EXTRACT FROM REGULATIONS OF THE MILITARY FUND LATELY CALLED
LORD CLIVE'S.

WIDOWS' PENSIONS.
  1. 1. Officers' widows will be required to sign a declaration to the effect that their husbands did not die possessed of real and personal property to the following amounts, and that they themselves do not, with what their husbands left and from all other sources possess, if the widow of a colonel, £4,000; a lieut.-colonel, £3,000; a major, £2,500; a captain, £2,000; a lieutenant £1,000.
  2. 2. The rates of pension are as follows:—
RANKS. Widows' Pensions.
Per dm.
s. d.		 Per annum.
£ s. d.		 Per dm.
s. d.		 Per annum.
£ s. d.
Colonel, Surgeon-General, and Deputy Surgeon-General 6 3 114 1 3 1 3
Lieut.-Colonel, Brigade-Surgeon, and Surgeon-Major after 20 years' service 5 0 91 5 0 0
Major, Surgeon-Major, and Chaplain of 15 years' service 3 9 68 8 9 9
Captain, Surgeon, Chaplain of 7 years' service, and Veterinary Surgeon of 20 years' service 2 6 45 12 6
Chaplain under 7 years' service 1 9 31 18 9
Lieutenant and Veterinary Surgeon 1 3 22 16 3
  1. 3. The pensions continue only during widowhood.
  2. 4. Pensions lapsing on re-marriage are not restored on a second widowhood.
  3. 5. Pensioners neglecting to claim the pension for three half-years will be considered as dead, and no arrears for a longer period than two years back from the date of application for admission or re-admission, as the case may be, will be allowed either to claimants or to pensioners after admission.
  4. 6. The widows of officers formerly of the Indian service, who have joined the British army, retain their claims to pension from this Fund.
  5. 7. The widows of officers who have retired from the service are not eligible to the benefits of this Fund.*
  6. 8. The widows of officers who at the time of their death were pensioners on Lord Clive's Fund are entitled to the benefits of the Fund, provided they were married to their husbands prior to the latter having become pensioners on such Fund.
  7. 9. These pensions are payable quarterly, on the 1st January, 1st April, 1st July, and 1st October.

INDIA OFFICE, March 1883.

Note.—Applications for admission to this Fund should be addressed to
The Military Secretary, India Office, London, S.W.

INDIAN MARINE.

All first appointments of Officers and Engineers in the Indian Marine are made by the Secretary of State for India in Council.

The limits of age for appointment to the junior executive rank, that of Third Grade Officer, are 17 and 22 years, and no candidate will be appointed who does not possess a Board of Trade certificate as Second Mate.

Applications for appointment can be recorded at the India Office, but will not be submitted for the orders of the Secretary of State until the applicant is duly qualified, and there is a vacancy.

When he is qualified, the Board of Trade Certificate, together with certificates of birth and character, should be transmitted to the Military Secretary, India Office.

Candidates must pass an examination by the India Office Medical Board prior to appointment.

On appointment a first-class passage to Bombay will be provided, and an advance of pay not exceeding £25, recoverable in India, will be made in aid of outfit.

Pay commences from date of embarkation for India. Service for pension reckons from date of arrival in India.

The names of candidates will be removed from the list on their attaining the age of 22 years.

Any further information relative to the appointment of executive Officers may be obtained on application to the Military Secretary, India Office.

Information in regard to the appointment of Assistant Engineer may be obtained from the Director General of Stores, India Office.

INDIA OFFICE

June, 1887.

* Except in the case of the widows of General Officers compulsorily retired under the provisions of the Royal Warrants of 31st December 1877, 1st May 1878, 25th June 1881, 11th March 1882, and 10th June 1884.

FURLOUGH AND LEAVE RULES OF THE INDIAN MARINE.

MARINE CIRCULAR OF THE GOVERNMENT OF INDIA, NO. 16 OF 1884.

Furlough.

I.—Officers after five years' service in India or under the Government of India may receive one year's furlough, and an additional year for each subsequent five years' service. Furlough not taken at the time it becomes due is not lost, and can, subject to these regulations, be taken later.

II.—An officer may not be absent from duty on any grounds for more than three years consecutively, nor on return from furlough may he receive, except under Rule VII., any further furlough until after an interval of three years.

III.—Furlough may be spent anywhere, and, when it has been earned by length of service, counts as service for pension. Furlough taken before it is earned by length of service reckons against service for pension till paid off.

IV.—An officer on furlough receives half the pay of his rank, or, if holding a shore appointment, half the pay of his appointment, excluding command, charge, acting, personal, or house allowance, computed according to a rate of exchange to be fixed in each year by the Secretary of State. An officer promoted while on furlough will be entitled to a moiety of the increased pay due to that promotion.

V.—An officer proceeding on or returning from furlough may be granted an advance of three months' furlough pay; and he may further be granted the advance in aid of passage money specified in clause IX., if he does not obtain passage by troopship. Advances made in England will be recovered in India by monthly instalments of one-fourth of the pay drawn by the officers. Advances made in India will be noted on the last pay certificates of officers, and no issue of furlough pay will be made till after the sum advanced has accrued as furlough pay.

VI.—Furlough pay may not be drawn for more than two years consecutively.

VII.—Upon certificate of a medical board, an officer not entitled to furlough, who has served not less than one year in India, may be granted furlough. Such furlough will not count as service for pension until it has been paid off by subsequent service under the terms of Rule I. Furlough cannot be anticipated beyond two years in the aggregate, nor for a second term, unless that previously anticipated has been subsequently paid off under the terms of Rule I.

VIII.—An officer who has exhausted the furlough obtainable under these rules, and who may be certified by a medical board to be still unfit for duty, will be retired on the pension or gratuity to which he may be entitled; if not entitled to either pension or gratuity, his case will be specially considered.

IX.—**Marine officers not holding shore appointments of Rs. 1,000 per mensem will be eligible for the grant of non-regulation passages in troopships when such passages are available. Officers who do not obtain passage in troopships will be allowed advances in aid of passage-money, in addition to the advances admissible under Clause V., provided that no officer shall in any case draw advances exceeding three months' pay in all.

X.—Furlough, whether on private affairs or on medical certificate, is restricted to a maximum period of one year in the first instance, and to subsequent extensions not exceeding six months at a time.

General Leave.

XI.—General leave may be granted for any period not exceeding six months at a time, but cannot be renewed, except for very urgent reasons, till after two years have elapsed from its expiration. It may be spent anywhere, does not involve forfeiture of appointment and counts as service for pension. It will be granted or refused entirely at the discretion of the Director of the Indian Marine. It may be granted up to a maximum limit of 30 days as subsidiary to furlough, whether on proceeding on furlough or on return from furlough. Leave granted as subsidiary to furlough can only be spent in India.

XII.—During general leave an officer will be restricted to the pay of his grade,* and will forfeit all allowances, which will be paid to his locum tenens. If an officer exceeds the six months' leave admissible at one time under this rule, the entire period will be converted into furlough, and his allowances adjusted accordingly.

Privilege Leave.

XIII.—Subject to the exigencies of the service, Marine officers may be allowed 60 days' privilege leave during each year, and such leave may be taken in instalments.

XIV.—An officer who has privilege leave to his credit when proceeding on leave or furlough does not forfeit it, but privilege leave cannot be taken in continuation of furlough, nor can furlough be obtained in continuation of privilege leave. Privilege leave may, however, be converted into general leave with the sanction of the Director of the Indian Marine, or into furlough with the sanction of the Government of India.

XV.—Privilege leave will be granted by the Director of the Indian Marine, but it will be granted only on the understanding that no additional expense is thereby caused to the State either directly or indirectly, that the applicant's services can be spared, and that satisfactory arrangements for the performance of his duties during his absence can be made.

XVI.—Officers are at liberty to proceed wherever they may desire when on privilege leave, provided they can ensure their return before the expiration of the leave. If an officer exceeds his privilege leave, the whole period of his absence will be converted into general leave or furlough, and his allowances and statement of service for furlough adjusted accordingly.

* This rule will also hold good in the case of an officer holding a shore appointment, in which case the balance will be considered a staff allowance payable to the locum tenens, provided the latter does not thereby draw more than the pay of the appointment.

† As modified by Marine Circular No. 6 of 1885.

§ As modified by Marine Circular No. 7 of 1886.

** As modified by Marine Circular No. 31 of 1885.

Temporary Leave.

XVII.—Temporary leave not exceeding 10 days, and not in anticipation or in extension of any other kind of leave or furlough, may be granted by the Director and Deputy Director of the Indian Marine.

XVIII.—Temporary leave not exceeding 72 hours may be granted by commanders of vessels and heads of departments to officers under their command.

General Regulations.

XIX.—It must be distinctly understood that furlough or leave cannot be claimed as a right. Both are subject to the exigencies of the public service, and will be granted only when an officer's services can be spared. Commanders of vessels and heads of departments will not forward an application for furlough or leave unless they are able to satisfy the Director of the Indian Marine that the officer's services can be spared without any inconvenience to the public service.

XX.—No grant of leave given under the conditions of these rules will count against an officer's service for pension, and leave may be spent anywhere, i.e., within possible limits of return before it expires.

XXI.—Leave is reckoned from the day on which an officer avails himself of it, and it expires on the day he rejoins.

Furlough reckons from date of leaving India or from date of leaving his ship if at an outstation, unless specially stated by authority to commence on an earlier date; and in the event of an officer returning to India before the expiration of his furlough, it ceases on the date of his return.

The day of return, whether from leave or from furlough, is a day of duty.

XXII.—Officers on furlough will draw their pay from the India Office, but officers on general or privilege leave must arrange to draw their pay in India.

XXIII.—As a rule, leave will be granted only during the period of the south-west monsoon.

XXIV.—Officers proceeding on furlough will report their departure personally at the office of the Director or Deputy Director of the Indian Marine, except when they avail themselves of it at an outstation. An officer returning from furlough or leave will rejoin the ship to which he belongs; but if this is impracticable, and he has not received instructions (which he should apply for beforehand), he will rejoin at Bombay or Calcutta, reporting himself in person to the Director or Deputy Director. On the departure of an officer on furlough or leave, or on the return of an officer therefrom, the commander of the vessel will cause an entry to be made of the date in the ship's log book, and will forward an extract thereof by the next post to the Director.

XXV.—Officers arriving in Europe on furlough must report their arrival in writing to the Military Secretary at the India Office, and state their address; and for extension of furlough or for permission to return to duty, they must also apply to the Military Secretary.

Officers proceeding to the Colonies on furlough should apply to India for extensions of furlough and for permission to return to duty.

XXVI.—During the absence of an officer on leave or furlough, he must keep the Director of the Indian Marine acquainted with his actual address, and be prepared to return immediately to India, should his services be required. When on leave, such recall order will be issued by the Director; when on furlough in Europe, by the Secretary of State. Officers spending their furlough in Europe must keep the Secretary of State informed of their address.

XXVII.—Applications for leave or furlough must be made on the authorized form, which is obtainable from the Director or Deputy Director of the Indian Marine.

NOTE.—(Marine Circular No. 10 of 1855.)

The above rules are applicable to—

I.—Officers on the Graded and supernumerary List of Executive Officers.

II.—Officers on the Graded List of Engineers.

III.—Gazetted clerks.

IV.—Warrant officers who may be specially admitted to the Rules.

PENSION REGULATIONS OF THE INDIAN MARINE.

MARINE CIRCULAR OF THE GOVERNMENT OF INDIA, NO. 13 OF 1855.

1. The following is the scale of pensions and compassionate allowances admissible to Officers and Engineers of Her Majesty's Indian Marine and to their families.

2. The service for pension of an officer of the Marine reckons from date of joining his ship or appointment if he is 19 years of age or over; otherwise from date of attaining that age.

3. An officer admitted to the service after 25 years of age will be subject to reduction of pension in the manner and under the rules laid down in the Civil Pension Code.

4. Retirement at the age of 55 years will be compulsory, and all Officers and Engineers of that age now in the service, as well as those who may attain that age within one year, will be required to retire at the expiration of one year from the date of this order. Should an officer holding the appointment of Director or Deputy Director of the Indian Marine attain that age during his incumbency, he may, with the approval of the Government of India, complete five years in the appointment.

[This rule will not be applicable to those officers who have already received a special extension of tenure.]

5. The following is the scale of pensions:—

Designation. Officers invalided after 3 and under 15 years' service owing to disease contracted in and by the service. After 15 years' service if invalided, and 20 years without invaliding. After 25 years' service if invalided. After 30 years' service, inclusive of the periods of furlough allowed to count as service for pension.
After 3 years' service. After 6 years' service. After 10 years' service.
Per Diem. Per mensem.
Rs. A. P. Rs. A. P. Rs. A. P. Rs. Rs. Rs.
Commanders, 1st grade ... .. } ... .. } ... .. } 5 8 0 270 410 490
Commanders, 2nd grade ... .. 220 330 400
Commanders, 3rd grade ... .. 190 280 340
First Grade Officers of six years' seniority. 140 210 260
First Grade Officers of three years' seniority. } ... .. } 4 0 0 120 170 210
First Grade Officers under three years' seniority. 90 130 160
Second Grade Officers ... .. } 1 12 0 } 2 4 0 80 120 150
Third Grade Officer ... .. 70 110 130
Chief Engineers on Rs. 400 per mensem. } ... .. } ... .. } 5 8 0 210 320 380
Chief Engineers on Rs. 300 per mensem. 180 270 320
Engineers of eight years' service in that grade. } ... .. } ... .. } 4 0 0 150 23 280
Engineers of four years' service in that grade. 140 200 250
Engineers under four years' service in that grade. 130 190 230
Assistant Engineers ... .. 1 12 0 2 4 0 2 8 0 100 160 190
* }

* The pension in no case to exceed one-third of the emoluments drawn by the officer when invalided.

The periods of service mentioned in the foregoing table are inclusive of the periods of furlough allowed to count towards pension.

6. The following extra pension will be granted to an officer of the Indian Marine retiring after five years' service as Deputy Director or Director:—

For five years' continuous service as Director ... .. Rs. 2,000
For five years' continuous service as Deputy Director ... .. 1,000
For five years' continuous service, partly as Deputy Director and partly as Director, of which not less than two years shall have been as Director ... .. 1,500

7. Service in the survey, or in port or dockyard appointments, gives no claim to any higher pension than that fixed for an officer's rank in the Marine. Officers not on the Marine list now serving in such appointments will remain under their present rules.

8. The pensions of warrant officers, gunners, and clerks will be calculated under the rules for the Un-covenanted Service, as laid down in the Civil Pension Code, Rs. 60 per mensem (compensation in lieu of board and lodging) being added to their pay for the calculation of pension.

9. Pensions will be payable in rupees in India. If paid in England, they will be converted at the rate of exchange fixed yearly for the adjustment of financial transactions between the Imperial and Indian Governments.

10. An officer may be allowed to commute a portion, not exceeding one-third, of his pension, the commuted value being payable in India under the rules in clause 23, India Army Circulars of 1884.†

11. Compassionate allowances on the following scale will be granted to the families of officers who may be drowned or meet with a violent death in the execution of duty:—

Widow. Child.
Commander ... .. } £100 a year, or Rs. 100 a month. } £20 a year, or Rs. 20 a month.
Port Officer ... ..
Chief Engineer ... ..
First Grade Officer ... ..
Engineer ... ..
Second Grade Officer ... .. } £75 a year, or Rs. 75 a month. } £15 a year, or Rs. 15 a month.
Third Grade Officer ... ..
Assistant Engineer ... ..
} £50 a year, or Rs. 50 a month. } £10 a year, or Rs. 10 a month.

The cases of the families of the Director or Deputy Director will be specially considered.

12. The regulations and conditions under which all such compassionate allowances are to be awarded will be analogous to those prescribed for the military service.

Officers are required to signify their intention to accept the above pension rules by the 31st December, 1885, otherwise it will be understood that they have decided to remain under the rules now applicable to them.

INDIAN MILITARY SERVICE FAMILY PENSION REGULATIONS.

(Instituted 1st Jan. 1873.)

REGULATIONS FOR THE PROVISION OF PENSIONS FOR THE WIDOWS AND ORPHANS OF MILITARY, MEDICAL, AND ECCLESIASTICAL OFFICERS IN THE SERVICE OF THE GOVERNMENT OF INDIA.

1. Pensions will be provided under these regulations for the widows and orphans of the following officers on the Indian establishment, not being entitled to the benefits of any of the late Military and Medical Funds :

  • Officers of the Staff Corps.
  • Officers of the Indian Medical Department.
  • Chaplains.

3. Every European officer who enters the service on and after the 1st January 1873, must, as a condition of his appointment, provide pensions for his family under these regulations. A Native Commissioned Medical Officer will be required to declare his intention of joining the Fund or not within three months from the date of his taking up his appointment in India.

Note—On appointment to the Service, Medical Officers and Chaplains are admitted to the Fund "from date of arrival in India."

5. For the purposes of these regulations officers are classed as follows:—

  • Class I.—Lieutenant-Colonels in receipt of Colonel's allowances, and Surgeons-General.
  • " II.—Lieutenant-Colonels, Archdeacons, Deputy Surgeons-General, Brigade Surgeons, and Surgeons-Major of 20 years' service.
  • " III.—Majors, Senior Chaplains, and Surgeons-Major.
  • " IV.—Captains, Junior Chaplains, and Surgeons who have served the Government of India for more than six years.
  • " V.—Lieutenants and Surgeons who have served the Government of India for less than six years.

6. The pensions provided under these regulations are payable monthly in India, and quarterly in arrears in England, and to date of death.

7. The amounts of the pensions provided are as follows, in addition to any pensions or compassionate allowances granted under Royal Warrant.

(N.B.—The one now in force is dated 10th June 1884.)

TABLE I.—Pension to a Widow during Widowhood, according to the Class of her Husband at the time of his death.

CLASS OF HUSBAND AT DEATH.
I. II. III. IV. V.
Amount of pension to widow per ann.: £ £ £ £ £
In Europe ..... 160 130 100 70 40
Rs. Rs. Rs. Rs. Rs.
In India ..... 1,669 1,356 1,043 730 417

TABLE II.—Pensions to the Orphan Children of all Classes alike.

From birth till age of 6 years. From age of 6 till age of 12 years. From age of 12 till age of 21 years. To females only from age of 21 years for life, or till marriage.
Amount of pension to each orphan: £ £ £ £
In Europe ..... 10 20 30 45
Rs. Rs. Rs. Rs.
In India ..... 104 208 313 469

8. The pension of a minor orphan will be paid to his or her mother, or other duly appointed or recognized guardian.

NOTE.—Annuitants in India must draw their pensions in Indian currency.

Contributions.

9. The contributions for pensions under these regulations, and the pensions of widows, will be regulated by the class of the contributing officer. An officer cannot secure a higher pension for his widow by contributing as if he belonged to a higher class.

* As an exception to this rule, an officer who has completed five years' service as a substantive Lieutenant-Colonel has the option of subscribing in Class I. (A. C. 34, of Jan. 1881.)

10. Contributions will be deducted by paymasters or other disbursing officers from the pay abstracts of officers contributing.

10A. The circumstance that an Officer is not in receipt of pay does not relieve him from the necessity of paying his contributions.

11. Donations may be paid in one sum, or in 12 equal monthly instalments. If a donation be paid in instalments, interest on the unpaid portion will be levied at the rate of 4½ per cent. per annum. The balance of any contribution due at an officer's death will be recovered rateably, without interest, from the pensions of his widow and orphans, in 12 monthly instalments in India, or four quarterly instalments in England.

11A. Contributions which become due from subscribers when in Europe must be paid in England in sterling. If a subscriber, previous to quitting India, has commenced the payment of any donation by instalments, he may either complete the payment of the donation in India before leaving, or pay the remaining instalments in England at English rates; but an Officer on half-pay who continues to subscribe at Indian rates has the option of paying in India.

12. The following are the rates of donations and monthly contributions prescribed for the present:—

TABLE III.—Donations.
Class of Contributors. On entering the Service of the Government of India married, or on marriage, or re-marriage. On promotion to a higher class.
In India. In Europe. In India. In Europe.
Rs. £ Rs. £
Class I. .... 4320 384 810 72
" II. .... 2160 192 405 36
" III. .... 1080 96 270 24
" IV. .... 540 48 135 12
" V. .... 270 24 — —

Disparity of Age.

If the age of the husband exceed that of the wife by 12 years or more, then the marriage donation shall be regulated by a disparity table (TABLE IV.), which can be obtained from the Director of Funds, India Office.

[G. G. O., No. 728 of 1875.]

(A.C.I. 1880 Cl. 132.)

Rs. A. £ s. Rs. A. £ s. d.
Class I. .... 54 0 2 10 27 0 1 4 0

NOTE.—The Europe rates of subscription are payable after retirement and during furlough out of India, and whenever full Indian pay is not received. But an officer on furlough in India, and not in receipt of full Indian pay, will be required to pay only half the Indian rate of contribution. (A. C. I. 1886. Cl. 9.)

TABLE VII.—Additional Monthly Contribution, payable by all Classes alike, for each Child living on the First Day of the Month.

In India. In Europe.
RS. A. P. £ s. d.
For a son, until age of 21 ..... 1 1 0 0 2 0
For a daughter, until marriage..... 2 11 0 0 5 0

Arrears.

13. Interest at the rate of 4½ per cent. per annum will be charged on arrears. Donations will become due from the date on which the liability is incurred, viz., on marriage, on promotion, or on the birth of a child, but interest will not be charged on sums paid within one month from the date on which they become due.

Reports of Marriages, Births, and Deaths.

14. An officer who is married at the date of his admission into the service must report the fact of his marriage at the time, and the names and dates of birth of his children, if any have been born to him. An officer in the service must report his marriage, or the birth of a child born in wedlock, or the death of a wife or child, within one month of the occurrence, to the Examiner of Fund Accounts in the Presidency, or the Director of Funds, India Office, when an officer is in Europe. Forms for this purpose are given in the Appendix.

15. The birth of every child born alive must be reported; but if a child die before it is thirty days old, no payment in respect of it will be required.

15A. If the report of a marriage, or of the birth of a child, be not made within one month of the occurrence, the donation payable in each case will be increased 2½ per cent., and interest thereon charged from the date of marriage or birth of the child. After six months' delay, donations will be increased 5 per cent., and interest charged in the usual manner. (G.O.G.G. No. 381 of 1877.)

15B. In the event of the report of a marriage or the birth of a child not being made by a subscriber during his lifetime, in accordance with the rules, the widow or child will not be entitled to any benefits. (Army Circulars, India, 1860, Clause II.)

Posthumous Child.

16. A pension will be granted to a posthumous child on payment of the donation prescribed in Table V., which may be recovered by 12 monthly instalments in India, or four quarterly instalments in England. The report of the birth of a posthumous child must be made in Form D., the last sentence being omitted.

Commutation of Contributions.

17. An officer who desires to complete his payments during the term of his service in India, so as to be exempt from contributions after his retirement, will be permitted to do so at rates to be determined by the tables appended to General Hannyington's report, dated London, 7th June, 1870, or by any other tables that may be hereafter substituted by the Secretary of State for India in Council.

18. An officer may at any time commute his future payments into a single payment, the amount of such payment being regulated by the same tables.

Duration of Pensions.

19. If a widow remarry, her pension will cease during coverture, but if she again become a widow she will be entitled to revert to her pension as widow of her first husband, or, if her last husband have been a contributor, to receive a pension at her option as the widow of her last husband. A widow can only receive one pension.

20. The pension of a male orphan is payable until he attains the age of 21 years.

21. The mother or guardian of a male orphan who has completed the age of 16 may, at any time thereafter (on application in Forms F. to H. annexed to these regulations), have the residue of his pension commuted into a single payment, for the due application of which the mother or guardian will be held responsible. The following table gives the commuted values of such pensions:—

TABLE VIII.—Showing the present Value of the Pension of a Male Orphan, according to his Age.

Years. 16 17 18 19 20
Months. £ £ £ £ £
0 129 106 81 56 28
3 123 100 75 49 21
6 117 94 68 42 14
9 111 87 62 35 7

22. The pension of a female orphan continues until her marriage, and then ceases absolutely. It does not revive on widowhood.

Good Conduct.

23. The pensions provided under these regulations shall, in all cases, be held during good behaviour only, and will be withheld if any pensioner is found to be leading an immoral life, or on proof of any gross misconduct. The order of the Secretary of State for India in Council in any such case shall be final and conclusive.

Alienation of Pension.

24. A pension paid under these regulations may not be transferred or assigned, or in any way alienated, from the pensioner to whom or for whose benefit it is payable.

Passage Money.

25. An officer may, by a special contribution, provide passage money from India for his widow and orphans. The amount so secured will belong to the officer's estate, but will be primarily applicable to the provision of passage money and outfit for his widow and children. The amount secured must not exceed 1,500 rupees, and a health certificate must in every case be furnished by the subscriber. The value will be determined by the Tables in Major-General Hannington's report.

25A. The special contribution can be paid by monthly instalments not exceeding 24, the instalments in arrear bearing interest at 4\frac{1}{2} per cent. per annum, and in the event of a subscriber dying before completion of the payment, the balance due at his death will be deducted from the sum insured.

25B. Native commissioned medical officers are required to pay, as in the case of their ordinary contributions, 5 per cent. over and above the ordinary rates of special contribution (Army Circulars India, 1884, Clause 33).

25C. In the event of a subscriber's family not having occasion to make use of the passage money for which special contribution has been made, it will be paid to his estate on his death, irrespective of whether this occurs before or after his retirement, or whether he continued to subscribe to the pension branch up to the date of his death.

If an officer on retirement desires to sever his connection with the Insurance Branch, he will be allowed the option of receiving the value of the sum assured at the date of withdrawal; in which case the value of the sum assured will be determined by the tables in the Regulations according to the member's age; e.g., a member withdrawing at the age of thirty, who has paid the special contribution to secure a sum of Rs. 1,500, will be entitled to receive on withdrawal:—\text{Rs. } 1,500 \times .391574 \times 1.032 = \text{Rs. } 600.

If, however, the option is declined at the date of withdrawal from the pension branch, the sum originally assured will be paid in accordance with the rules at the decease of the subscriber. (A. C. I. 1886. Cl. 85.)

Transfer to Her Majesty's Line Regiments, and Officers who resign.

26. Officers who may be transferred from the Indian Staff Corps to Her Majesty's Line regiments, and officers who resign the service, will be entitled to receive back such portion of their contributions as may be in excess of the risk borne during their membership, but they cannot continue subscribers.

Half-Pay.

27. Married officers placed on half-pay may receive back such portion of their contributions as is in excess of the risk borne during the term of their membership, or they may continue to subscribe at Indian rates (which can be paid in India) for full benefits, or at lower rates for reduced benefits. In the event of their return to full pay, they shall, in addition to the subscription of their rank, be required to pay such donation as the circumstances of the case may demand. Unmarried officers may continue to subscribe at the Europe rate of subscription, and will not be entitled to any refund in the event of their withdrawal.

Retired Officers.

28. Married officers who retire from the service on a pension may continue to subscribe at the Europe rate of subscription, or they may withdraw, receiving back such portion of their contributions as is in excess of the risk borne during the term of their membership; but the usual subscription will be deducted from the pension of all such officers until they shall have intimated in writing their intention of withdrawing. Unmarried officers may continue their subscription on the same terms as married officers, but will not be entitled on retirement to any refund in the event of their withdrawal.

Dismissed Officers.

29. An officer who is dismissed the service by sentence of a court martial, or by other competent authority, will cease, ipso facto, to be a contributor under these regulations, and will have no claim to any refund. His family will not be entitled to any pensions under these regulations.

Certificates.

30. Declarations as to continued widowhood and the survival of children must be furnished to the Accountant-General, India Office, by whom pensions in Europe are paid under the authority of the Director of Funds.

Note A.—The contributions and donations to be paid by Native Members of the Covenanted Medical Service are fixed for the present at an increase of 5 per cent. on the rates paid by Europeans.

APPENDIX.

Note.—In England all reports and applications for pensions must be made to the Director of Funds, India Office, London, S.W.

For the present, the rate of interest is fixed at 4\frac{1}{2} per cent. per annum, and the rate of exchange with England at 1s. 8d. the rupee.

FORM (B.) of a Letter from an Officer reporting his having joined the service of the Government of India married. (See Regulation 14.)

To the Examiner of Fund Accounts for the Presidency of

Sir,—With reference to the Family Pension Regulations published with the Notification by the Government of India, in the Military Department, No. 1315, dated 28th December, 1872, I have the honour to report that I was admitted to the service of the Government of India, married, on the

I was born on the

My wife was born on the

We were married on the

A certified copy of the register of our marriage is herewith submitted.

The names and birthdays of my children are as follows:—

lxxxix

FORM (D.) of a Letter reporting the Birth of a Child. (See Regulations 14 and 15)

To the Examiner of Fund Accounts for the Presidency of
Sir,—I have the honour to report the birth to me of a male (female) child on the may
and I request that the name be entered in the pension register kept under the regulations published with Notification by the Government of India, in the Military Department, No. 1315, dated 28th December 1872, and that the deduction thereby prescribed may be made from my pay.

FORM (E.)—Report of Death. (See Regulation 14.)

To the Examiner of Fund Accounts for the Presidency of
Sir,—I have the honour to report the decease on the of my son
(daughter) born on the A death certificate is herewith
(or of my wife) transmitted.

FORM (F.) of application for the commuted value of the pension of a Male Orphan over 16 years of age (See Regulation 21.)

Sir,—I, A. B., being the mother (or guardian) of C. D., son of E. F., late a in the service of the Government of India, having now an opportunity of placing my son (or ward) in a suitable position, request that the commuted value of the remaining portion of his pension may be granted to me under my guarantee for its proper application to his use.
The age of my son (or ward) is now years months, and I submit herewith a certificate of his health.

FORM (G.).—Medical Certificate to be furnished by Mother or Guardian applying for commutation of a Male Orphan's Annuity. (See Regulation 21.)

We do hereby certify that we have carefully and personally examined into the state of the health of C. D., son of the late E. F., and that we consider him free from any disease likely to prevent his attaining the age of 21 years.

(Sd.) G. H. } M.D.
I. K. }

FORM (H.).—Mother or Guardian's Receipt for Commuted Value of a Male Orphan's Annuity. (See Regulation 21.)

I, A. B., mother (or guardian) of C. D., son of the late E. F., in consideration of the payment to me of the sum of being the commuted value of the pension receivable by the said C. D., from
till he attains the age of 21 years, do hereby bind myself to account, if called on by the Secretary of State for India, for the due application of the said sum for the use and benefit of the said C. D.

WARRANT OF PRECEDENCE IN INDIA.

VICTORIA, by the grace of God, of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland, Queen Defender of the Faith, Empress of India,—

To all to whom these presents shall come greeting

WHEREAS it hath been represented unto Us that it is advisable that the rank and precedence of persons holding appointments in the East Indies, as regulated by Our Royal Warrant, dated the 6th day of May, 1871, should be altered in manner hereinafter mentioned, We do therefore hereby declare that it is Our Will and Pleasure, notwithstanding anything in Our said Warrant contained, that the following Table be henceforth observed with respect to the rank and precedence of the persons hereinafter named, viz:—

  1. 1. Governor-General and Viceroy of India.
  2. 2. Governors of Madras and Bombay.
  3. 3. President of the Council of the Governor-General.
  4. 4. Lieutenant-Governor of Bengal, the North-Western Provinces, or the Punjab, when in his own territories.
  5. 5. Commander-in-Chief in India.
  6. 6. Lieutenant-Governors of Bengal, the North-Western Provinces, and the Punjab.
  7. 7. Chief Justice of Bengal.
  8. 8. Bishop of Calcutta, Metropolitan of India.
  9. 9. Ordinary Members of the Council of the Governor-General.
  10. 10. Commanders-in-Chief in Madras and Bombay.
  11. 11. Commander-in-Chief of Her Majesty's Naval Forces in India, unless senior in relative rank to the Commander-in-Chief of Madras and Bombay, in which case he will take the precedence.
  12. 12. Chief Justices of Madras, Bombay, and the North-Western Provinces.
  13. 13. Bishops of Madras and Bombay.
  14. 14. Ordinary Members of Council in Madras and Bombay.
  15. 15. Chief Commissioners and Resident at Hyderabad, and Agents to the Governor-General in Rajputana, Central India, and Baroda.
  16. 16. Puisne Judges of the High Courts of Calcutta, Madras, Bombay, and the North-Western Provinces.
  17. 17. Military Officers above the rank of Major-General.
  18. 18. Additional Members of the Council of the Governor-General, when assembled to make Laws, &c.
  19. 19. Secretaries to the Government of India.
  20. 20. Commissioner in Sind.
  21. 21. Judges of the Chief Court, Punjab.
  22. 22. Additional Members of the Councils of the Governors of Madras and Bombay, when assembled to make Laws, &c.

23. Chief Secretaries to the Governments of Madras and Bombay.

24. Members of the Legislative Council of the Lieutenant-Governor of Bengal.

25. Vice-Chancellors of Indian Universities.

FIRST CLASS.

26. Civilians of 31 years' standing and Major-Generals.

27. Advocate-General, Calcutta.

28. Advocates-General, Madras and Bombay.

29. Members of the Boards of Revenue, Madras, Bengal, and the North-Western Provinces, and Commissioners of Revenue and Customs, Bombay.

30. Financial Commissioner, Punjab.

31. Judicial Commissioners, and the Recorder of Rangoon.

32. Comptroller-General of Accounts in India.

33. Commissioners of Divisions within their own Divisions, and Residents, Political Agents, and Superintendents on pay of Rs. 2,000 per mensem or more (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts) within their own charges.—

34. Civil and Military Secretaries to Governments, Madras and Bombay, and Civil Secretaries to Governments, Bengal, North-Western Provinces, and Punjab.

35. Surveyor-General of India, and Directors-General of the Post Office, and of Telegraphs.

36. Chief Engineers, 1st Class.

37. Archdeacons of Calcutta, Madras and Bombay.

38. Brigadiers-General.

SECOND CLASS.

39. Civilians of 23 years' standing and Colonels.

40. Commissioners of Divisions and Commissioners of Police, Calcutta.

41. Private Secretary to the Viceroy.

42. Residents, Political Agents, and Superintendents on pay of Rs. 2,000 per mensem or more (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts).

43. Superintendent, Great Trigonometrical Survey.

44. Commissioner of Inland Customs.

45. Sanitary Commissioner with the Government of India.

46. Superintendent of the Geological Survey.

47. Inspector-General of Forests in India.

48. Standing Counsel to the Government of India.

49. Military Accountant-General.

50. Directors of Public Instruction under Local Governments.

51. Accountants-General for Local Governments.

52. Inspectors-General of Police under Local Governments.

53. Director of Revenue Settlement, and Superintendent of Revenue Survey, Madras; Survey and Settlement Commissioners, Bombay; Commissioner of Settlements, Punjab.

54. Remembrancers of Legal Affairs and Government Advocates in the North-Western Provinces, the Punjab, and British Burma.

55. Consulting Engineers to the Government of India for Guaranteed Railways, Calcutta and Lahore, and Chief Engineers, Second and Third Classes, under Local Governments.

56. District and Sessions Judges, Collectors and Magistrates of Districts, Deputy Commissioners of Districts, Deputy Superintendent of Port Blair, and the Chief Officer of each Presidency Municipality, within their respective charges.

57. Officers in the First Class, Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Covenanted Civil Service.

THIRD CLASS.

58. Civilians of 18 years' standing and Lieutenant-Colonels.

59. Political Agents and Superintendents on pay of Rs. 1,000 per mensem, but less than Rs. 2,000 (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts) within their own charges.

60. Military Secretary to the Government, Punjab, and Civil Secretaries to Local Administrations.

61. Private Secretaries to Governors.

62. Directors of Public Instruction under Local Administrations.

63. Administrators-General, Calcutta, Madras, and Bombay.

64. Inspectors-General of Jails and of Registration, Sanitary Commissioners, Inspectors and Conservators of Forests under Local Governments, and Postmasters-General.

65. Accountants-General for Local Administrations.

66. Consulting Engineer to the Government of India for Guaranteed Railways, Lucknow, and Chief and Superintending Engineers when Secretaries to Local Administrations, or to Agents to the Governor-General.

67. Inspectors-General of Police under Local Administrations.

68. Senior Chaplains.

69. Superintendent of Marine, Bombay, and Deputy Director of Indian Marine.

70. Master Attendants and Port Officers.

71. Sheriffs within their own charges.

72. Officers in the Second Class, Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Covenanted Civil Service.

FOURTH CLASS.

73. Civilians of 12 years' standing and Majors.

74. Political Agents and Superintendents on pay less than Rs. 1,000 per mensem, within their own charges.

75. Government Solicitors.

76. Inspectors-General of Jails and of Registration, Sanitary Commissioners, and Conservators of Forests under Local Administrations.

77. Officers in the Third Class, Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Covenanted Civil Service.

Officers in the above table will take precedence in order of the numbers of the entries. Those included in one number will take precedence inter se according to the date of entry into that number.

When an officer holds more than one position in the table he will be entitled to the highest position accorded to him.

Officers who are temporarily officiating in any number in the table will rank in that number below permanent incumbents.

All officers not mentioned in the above table, whose rank is regulated by comparison with rank in the army, to have the same rank with reference to civil servants as is enjoyed by Military Officers of equal grades.

All other persons who may not be mentioned in this table to take rank according to general usage, which is to be explained and determined by the Governor-General in Council in case any question shall arise.

Nothing in the foregoing Rules to disturb the existing practice relating to precedence at Native Courts, or on occasions of intercourse with Natives, and the Governor-General in Council to be empowered to make rules for such occasions in case any dispute shall arise.

All ladies to take place according to the rank herein assigned to their respective husbands, with the exception of wives of Peers, and of ladies having precedence in England independently of their husbands, and who are not in rank below the daughters of Barons, such ladies to take place according to their several ranks, with reference to such precedence in England, immediately after the wives of Members of Council at the Presidencies in India.

Given at Our Court at Balmoral, this eighteenth day of October, in the year of our Lord One thousand eight hundred and seventy-six, and in the fortieth Year of Our Reign.

By Her Majesty's Command.

(Signed) SALISBURY

WARRANT OF PRECEDENCE IN INDIA.SUPPLEMENTARY GRADED LIST OF CIVIL OFFICES NOT RESERVED FOR MEMBERS OF THE COVENANTED CIVIL SERVICE, PREPARED UNDER THE ORDERS OF THE GOVERNOR GENERAL IN COUNCIL.

First Judges of Presidency Courts of Small Causes.

Geological Survey Officers, 1st grade.

Government Astronomer, Madras.

Superintendent of Royal Botanical Gardens, Calcutta.

Superintendent of Marine Survey.

Superintendent of Inam Settlements, Mysore.

Superintendents of Revenue Survey and Assessment, Bombay and Mysore.

Superintending Engineers, Public Works Department.

Under Secretaries to the Government of India.

Commanders in the Indian Marine.

THIRD CLASS—(Number 77 of the Warrant).

Assistant Secretaries to the Government of India.

Deputy Commissioner, Inland Customs.

Deputy Commissioner of Police, Calcutta.

Deputy Directors of Revenue Settlements, and of Revenue Surveys, Madras.

Deputy Superintendents of Revenue, Topographical and Trigonometrical Surveys, 2nd grade.

Educational Department Officers, 2nd grade.

Examiners of Accounts, Public Works Department, 2nd class, 2nd grade.

Executive Engineers, Public Works Department, 1st grade.

Geological Survey Officers, 2nd grade.

Inspector-in-Chief, Cotton Frauds, Bombay.

Junior Judge of Presidency Courts of Small Causes.

Presidency Magistrates.

Registrars to the High Courts and to the Chief Court, Punjab.

Settlement Officer, Sind.

Superintendent of the Indian Museum.

Superintendents of Stamps and Stationery.

Superintendents, Telegraph Department, 1st and 2nd grades.

First Grade Officers of six years' seniority in the Indian Marine, and Chief Engineers in that service.

Keeper of the Records.—Mr. J. L. Turner.
Map Mounter.—Mr. H. Jones.
Press Writer.—Mr. W. Spence.
Assistant Press Writer.—Mr. E. Yeo.

INDIAN TROOP SERVICE vis-à-vis EGYPT.

The business of the Troop Service is under the superintendence of Vice-Admiral Sir Francis W. Sullivan, K.C.B., C.M.G., Director of Transports at the Admiralty.

Consulting Officer for the Indian Troop Service.—Captain A. W. Chitty, late I.N.

Resident Transport Officer (Suez), and Agent to the Government of India in Egypt.—Captain J. Brebner, late I.N.

Clerk at Suez.—C. Turner, Esq.

Resident Transport Officer (Bombay).—Capt. J. Hext, R.N.

Assistant do.—Capt. Sir H. Morland, late I.N.

ROYAL INDIAN ENGINEERING COLLEGE, COOPER'S HILLBOARD OF VISITORS.CHAIRMAN.

Sir Richard Temple, Bart., M.P., G.C.S.I., C.I.E., D.C.L.

MEMBERS

W. H. Barlow, Esq., F.R.S., Past President, Institution of Civil Engineers

Sir F. J. Bramwell, F.R.S., Past President, Institution of Civil Engineers

Lieut.-Gen. C. H. Dickens, C.S.I., R.A.

Sir J. Fowler, K.C.M.G., Past President, Institution of Civil Engineers

W. H. Preece, Esq., F.R.S., Member of Council, Institution of Civil Engineers.

Lieut.-Gen. R. Strachey, C.S.I., R.E., F.R.S.

Col. H. Yule, C.B., R.E.

Sir Dietrich Brandis, K.C.I.E.

STAFF OF THE COLLEGE.

President.—Gen. Sir A. Taylor, K.C.B., R.E.

Professor of Construction.—Callcott Reilly, Esq., M.I.C.E.

Professor of Hydraulic Engineering and Mechanism.—T. A. Hearson, Esq., Chf. Eng., R.N., Fellow Royal School of Naval Architecture.

Assistant Professor of Engineering.—A. H. Heath, Esq., Assoc. Inst. C.E.

Professor of Mathematics.—Rev. J. Wolstenholme, M.A., D.Sc.

Professor of Applied Mathematics.—G. M. Minchin, Esq., M.A.

Assistant Professor of Mathematics.—A. Lodge, Esq., M.A., Fellow of St. John's College, Oxon.

Professor of Surveying.—Col. E. H. Courtney, R.E.

Instructor in Surveying.—Lieut. T. J. W. Prendergast, R.E.

Professor of Chemistry.—Herbert McLeod, Esq., F.R.S.

Demonstrator in Chemistry.—F. E. Mathews, Esq., Ph.D.

Professor of Physics.—W. N. Stocker, Esq., M.A., Fellow of Brasenose College, Oxon.

Demonstrator in Physics.—W. G. Gregory, Esq., M.A.

Instructor in Geometrical Drawing and Lecturer in Architecture.—T. H. Eagles, Esq., M.A.

Assistant Instructor in Geometrical Drawing.—

Instructor in Freehand Drawing.—Russell Dowson, Esq.

Professor of Forestry.—W. Schlich, Esq., Ph.D., Inspector Genl. of Forests to Government of India

Professor of Botany.—H. Marshall Ward, Esq., M.A., Fellow of Christ's College, Cambridge.

Lecturer in Geology and Mineralogy.—Professor P. M. Duncan, M.B., F.R.S.

Lecturer in Accounts.—J. C. Hurst, Esq.

Demonstrator in the Mechanical and Laboratory.—P. Reilly, Esq.

Instructor in French.—F. W. Mariassy, Esq.

Instructor in German.—T. H. Dittel, Esq.

Bursar.—J. P. Pasco, Esq., R.N., Retired.

Secretary.—J. G. Whiffin, Esq., Paymaster-in-Chief, R.N., Retired

Chaplain.—Rev. C. Crosleigh, D.D.

THE MOST EXALTED ORDER OF THE
STAR OF INDIA,†

INSTITUTED 1861 ENLARGED 1866, 1875, and 1876

SOVEREIGN OF THE ORDER.

The QUEEN.

GRAND MASTER OF THE ORDER.

(His Excellency THE EARL OF DUFFERIN, G.C.B., K.P., G.C.M.G., G.M.I.E.), the
VICEROY and GOVERNOR GENERAL of INDIA for the time being.

THE MOST EMINENT
ORDER OF THE INDIAN EMPIRE.

The QUEEN.

The VICEROY and GOVERNOR GENERAL of INDIA for the time being
(His Excellency The EARL OF DUFFERIN, G.C.B., K.P., G.C.M.G., G.M.S.I.)

IMPERIAL ORDER OF THE CROWN OF INDIA.

INSTITUTED 1st JANUARY, 1878.
SOVEREIGN OF THE ORDER,—THE QUEEN.

LADIES OF THE ORDER.—C.I.
(With Dates of Appointments).

Her Royal Highness the Princess of Wales, 1 Jan. 78 Maharani Hai Shornomoyi, of Cossimbazar, 1 Jan. 78 [Jan. 78]

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA.

VICEROY AND GOVERNOR-GENERAL OF INDIA, The Right Hon. The EARL OF DUFFERIN, K.P., G.C.B., G.C.M.G., G.M.S.I., G.M.I.E.
Took his seat 13 Dec., 1884.

PERSONAL STAFF OF THE GOVERNOR-GENERAL.

Private Secretary.—Sir Donald Mackenzie Wallace, K.C.I.E.

Asst. Private Secretary.—D. Panioty, C.I.E.

Military Secretary.—Major Lord W. L. De la Poer Beresford, V.C., C.I.E., 9th Lancers

COUNCIL.

Extraordinary Member, Gen. Sir F. S. Roberts, Bart., G.C.B., G.C.I.E., V.C., R.A., Commander-in-Chief. Took his seat 28th November, 1885

Sir Theodore C. Hope, K.C.S.I., C.I.E. Took his seat 22nd July 82

Sir Auckland Colvin, K.C.M.G., C.I.E. Took his seat 11th October, 83

Andrew Richard Scoble, C.I.E., Q.C. Took his seat 1st December, 1886

Sir Charles U. Aitchison, K.C.S.I., C.I.E., LL.D., D.C.L. Took his seat 2nd April, 1887 —on furl.

HILL STATES.

Comprising Sirmur (Nahan), Kahlu (Bilaspur), Hindur (Nálagarh), Keonthal, Baghal, Baghat Jubbol, Kumharsain, Bhajji, Mailog, Balsan, Dhami, Kuthar, Kunhiar, Mangal, Bija, Darkoti, Tarhoch and Sāngri. The Deputy Commissioner of Simla is ex-officio Superintendent

FRONTIER TRIBES.

The Deputy Commissioners of Hazára Pesháwur, Kohát, Bannu, Dera Ismail Khan and Dera Ghazí Khan are Political Officers with respect to the Independent Tribes bordering on their respective Districts

DEATH.

Sir W. Elliot, K.C.S.I.

GOVERNOR, THE RIGHT HON. DONALD JAMES LORD REAY, LL.D., G.C.I.E.

TOOK HIS SEAT 30 MARCH, 1885.

PERSONAL STAFF.

Private Secretary—J. Monteath, M.A.
Military Secretary—Col. Hon. N. G. Lyttleton,
Rifle Brigade
Aide-de-Camp—Capt. A. W. B. Gordon, R.A.
Aide-de-Camp—Lieut. A. Phayre, 3rd Bombay
Cavalry

Extra Aide-de-Camp—Lieut. The Hon. H.
Yarde-Buller, 4th Bn., Rifle Brigade
Native Aide-de-Camp—Ressaidar Roop Sing, 2nd
Bombay Lancers.
Medical Officer—Surg.-Major L. A. Irving
Commandant of H.E. the Governor's Body-Guard
—Maj. M. Mayne, Bo.S.C.

COUNCIL.

Member, Lieut.-Gen. H.R.H. A. W. P. A. Duke
of Connaught and Strathearn, K.G., K.T., K.P.,
G.C.S.I., G.C.M.G., G.C.I.E., C.B., A.D.C.,
Commander-in-Chief, took his seat 14th Dec.,
1886—on leave

Member, James Braithwaite Peile, M.A., C.S.I.,
took his seat 23rd December, 1882
Member, Sir Maxwell Melvill, K.C.I.E., C.S.I.,
took his seat 8th April, 1884

B. Bengal; M. Madras; Bo. Bombay; C. Cavalry; I. Infantry; S.C. Staff Corps.

NOTE.—The names, in Italics, of Officers of the Staff Corps (who are borne for promotion on a separate List—See Page 202)—are retained upon this list for the purpose of regulating the promotion of Officers of the Indian Army (Local Service).

The Sphinx.

"Blenheim"—"Ramillies"—"Oudenarde"—"Malplaquet"—"Egypt"—"Peninsula"—"Sobraon"—
"Punjab"—"Mooltan"—"Googerat"—"Lucknow."

Scarlet—Facings White.

Arrived at Bombay from Gibraltar 24th January, 1882. Roorkee.

BRITISH INFANTRY.The Royal Welsh Fusiliers.(Late 23rd Foot.)1st BATTALION."Ich dien."

The Prince of Wales's plume.—The Rising Sun.—The Red Dragon.—The White Horse.—The Sphinx.
"Blenheim" — "Ramillies" — "Oudenarde" — "Malplaquet" — "Dettingen" — "Minden" — "Egypt" —
"Corunna" — "Martinique" — "Albuhera" — "Badajoz" — "Salamanca" — "Vittoria" — "Pyrences" —
"Nivelle" — "Orthes" — "Toulouse" — "Peninsula" — "Waterloo" — "Alma" — "Inkerman" —
"Sevastopol" — "Lucknow" — "Ashantee."

Scarlet—Facings Blue.

BRITISH INFANTRY.The East Lancashire Regiment.

FIRST BATTALION.

(Late 30th Foot.)

The Sphinx.

"Egypt" — "Cape of Good Hope, 1806" — "Corunna" — "Java" — "Badajoz" — "Salamanca" — "Vittoria"
— "St. Sebastian" — "Nive" — "Peninsula" — "Waterloo" — "Bhurtpore" — "Alma" — "Inkerman"
— "Sevastopol" — "Canton" — "Ahmad Khel" — "Afghanistan, 1878-80."

Scarlet—Facings White.

Arrived at Bombay from England 8th February 1880—Ferozepore.

GOVERNOR-GENERAL'S BODY-GUARD.

Dehra Dun. ]

Uniform, scarlet—Facings, dark blue.

"Java"—"Ava"—"Maharajpore"—"Moodkee"—"Ferozeshuhur"—"Aliwal"—"Sobraon."

COMMANDANT.

ERRATUM.—The date of the death of Maj.-Gen. T. Staples was 23rd Oct. 1886, not 25th, as previously stated.

MADRAS.

GOVERNOR,

The Right Hon. LORD CONNEMARA, G.C.I.E.

Took his seat 8th December, 1886.

BRITISH CAVALRY.XII. (Prince of Wales' Royal) Lancers.

The Plume of the Prince of Wales, the Rising Sun and the Red Dragon. "Egypt" (with The Sphinx)—
"Peninsula"—"Waterloo"—"South Africa, 1851-2-3"—"Sevastopol"—"Central India."

Uniform, Blue—Facings and Plume, Scarlet.

Arrived in India on the 31st January, 1877. Bangalore.—Arrived on the 13th March, 1882,
from Secunderabad.

BRITISH INFANTRY.

The Royal Fusiliers (City of London Regiment).

(Late 7th Foot.)

(SECOND BATTALION.)

The White Rose within the Garter, and the White Horse. "Martinique"—"Talavera"—"Albuhera"—
"Badajoz"—"Salamanca"—"Vittoria"—"Pyrenees"—"Orthes"—"Toulouse"—"Peninsula"—"Alma"—
"Inkerman"—"Sevastopol"—"Kandahar, 1880"—"Afghanistan, 1879-80."

Scarlet.—Facings, Blue.

Arrived in India 1st November 1873.

BOMBAY RETIRED OFFICERS.

(The names of General Officers of the Local Service retired under the Royal Warrant of 31st December, 1877, and of those of the Staff Corps and Royal (late Indian) Artillery and Engineers retired under the Royal Warrants of 13th August, 1877, 1st May, 1878, and 25th June, 1881, will be found at pages 200, 202, 204 and 205.)

INDEX.—ACTIVE SERVICE.

NOTE.—Native names will be found under the first portion of the name, omitting the following words when used as titles:—Babu, Bakhshi, Haji, Jemadar, Kazi, Khan Bahadur, Khan Sahib, Khwajah, Maharaiah, Maulvi, Mir, Mirza, Nawab, Pandit, Rai, Rai Bahadur, Rajah, Rao, Rao Bahadur, Rao Saheb, Seth, Shahzadah, Shaikh, Sirdar, Subadar, Syud, and Wazirzadah.
